departmentReports.summary: I recommend landing at Arcadia Planitia. From a medical and operational health perspective, both sites impose the same radiation burden, so the deciding factor is preventable injury risk and mission stability. At Mars surface dose rates of ~0.67 mSv/day, a 100-person colony accumulates about 367.1 mSv per person over the current 18-month food reserve horizon, which is a very high cumulative exposure burden regardless of landing site. Since radiation is equivalent at both locations, the safer terrain at Arcadia Planitia better protects colonists from landing accidents, rockfall trauma, construction injuries, and delayed medical access. Valles Marineris offers richer geology and mineral diversity, but the 30-degree slopes and canyon-edge hazards create avoidable acute medical risk that is not offset by a health advantage.

citations.text: Mars surface radiation averages 0.67 mSv/day, approximately 20x Earth background
citations.context: Used to estimate cumulative radiation exposure over the colony's 18-month reserve window.

citations.text: Arcadia Planitia contains extensive subsurface ice deposits detected by MARSIS radar
citations.context: Supports the site’s water-security and life-support resilience, reducing dehydration and logistics risk.

citations.text: Valles Marineris spans 4,000 km with up to 7 km depth, exposing 3.5 billion years of strata
citations.context: Explains the geological appeal but also underscores the scale and complexity of the canyon environment.

citations.text: CRISM spectrometer detected diverse hydrated minerals in Valles Marineris walls
citations.context: Confirms scientific value of Valles Marineris, but this is secondary to immediate settlement safety.

citations.text: Valles Marineris terrain slopes up to 30 degrees increase landing risk and construction difficulty
citations.context: Primary reason I judge the site to have higher acute injury and mission-failure risk.

risks.description: Radiation is already high on Mars; over 18 months at 0.67 mSv/day the colony would accrue about 367.1 mSv per person, increasing long-term cancer and tissue-damage risk.
Valles Marineris rim adds major acute hazard: slope instability, rockfall, and complex construction conditions increase the likelihood of traumatic injury and delayed rescue.
Arcadia Planitia is geologically less exciting, which may reduce morale for scientific teams, but this is manageable and not a medical hazard.
If settlement stability is compromised by terrain, food/water/power delivery interruptions could worsen malnutrition, dehydration, and secondary health decline.

opportunities.description: Arcadia Planitia’s subsurface ice improves water security, sanitation, and emergency reserves, which directly benefits colonist health.
A flatter landing zone should reduce EVA injury rates and conserve medical supplies and crew time.
The colony can still support science by sending robotic surveys or later field stations to Valles Marineris after the settlement is medically and logistically established.

recommendedActions: Select Arcadia Planitia for first permanent settlement.
Prioritize immediate shielding and storm shelter design to reduce cumulative radiation exposure.
Use subsurface ice access to secure water, oxygen, and hygiene capacity from day one.
Delay high-risk canyon operations until the colony has robust medical, construction, and evacuation capability.
Implement strict EVA fall-protection and trauma protocols regardless of landing site.
Monitor bone density, radiation biomarkers, and psychological stress from arrival onward.

forgedToolsUsed.name: mars_radiation_burden_model_run
forgedToolsUsed.description: Computes projected cumulative radiation dose over the remaining food reserve period and classifies the burden for the colony.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Dr. Yuki Tanaka
featuredColonistUpdates.update: Health outlook remains stable at baseline, but cumulative Mars radiation will become the dominant long-term medical risk after landing.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Erik Lindqvist
featuredColonistUpdates.update: No current injury, but site selection should minimize trauma risk because treatment capacity will be limited in the early settlement phase.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Amara Osei
featuredColonistUpdates.update: Psychological wellbeing is likely better supported by a safer, more predictable landing environment than by a high-hazard canyon rim.

openQuestions: What radiation shielding mass can be delivered before or shortly after landing?
Will the first habitat be partially buried or otherwise shielded against galactic cosmic rays and solar particle events?
How much redundancy exists in medical, water, and power systems if landing or construction is delayed?

departmentReports.reasoning: Arcadia Planitia is the safer and more buildable first settlement site. The science supports both sites having similar radiation exposure, so radiation does not differentiate them. Arcadia’s flat basalt plains dramatically reduce landing risk, site prep complexity, and early construction burden, which matters most for a first permanent outpost with limited margin. Its subsurface ice deposits are a major operational advantage for water, oxygen, and propellant logistics. Valles Marineris offers strong scientific value and mineral diversity, but the 30° slopes, rockfall risk, and large elevation changes create substantial mission risk and require terracing and more complex construction—poor trade for an initial settlement.

infrastructureAssessment.assessment: The colony is not yet resource-poor, but early settlement success depends on minimizing construction risk and maximizing access to water ice. Arcadia best supports that objective.

commanderDecision.decision: Land at Arcadia Planitia and immediately pivot the first settlement phase toward shielding, water extraction, and low-risk infrastructure buildup.
commanderDecision.rationale: We have one strong report and one silence. That means we do not freeze; we exploit the clearest asymmetric advantage on the table. Medical is right: radiation is brutal at either site, so terrain safety becomes the real differentiator at landfall. Arcadia gives us flatter ground, easier construction, faster logistics, fewer injury pathways, and cleaner expansion lanes. Valles Marineris is seductive—dramatic geology, rich mineral upside, bold frontier energy—but it is upside we can harvest later with dedicated expeditions. Right now, the colony wins by surviving cleanly through the vulnerable opening turns. Arcadia lets us build a durable base faster, preserve workforce capacity, and convert subsurface ice into water, oxygen, hygiene, and eventually shielding mass. That is not a timid choice; it is a launch platform. We secure the stable plain first, then project outward aggressively once systems are hardened.
selectedPolicies: Settle Arcadia Planitia as the primary landing and first permanent base site.
Declare Phase 1 construction priority: radiation shielding, storm shelter capacity, and habitat hardening.
Begin immediate subsurface ice survey and extraction setup to secure water and life-support inputs.
Restrict high-risk canyon and steep-slope operations until heavy rescue, medevac, and construction capability matures.
Establish strict EVA trauma prevention protocols from day one, including fall protection, route discipline, and rescue readiness.
Start continuous health monitoring for radiation exposure, bone density, and psychological stress at arrival.
rejectedPolicies: Land at Valles Marineris for early geology upside.
Prioritize scientific novelty over initial construction safety.
Conduct immediate canyon-edge expansion before emergency response systems are proven.
Delay shielding investment in favor of faster broad-footprint expansion.
expectedTradeoffs: We sacrifice early access to richer geological diversity and potentially faster mineral discovery in exchange for lower injury risk and faster safe buildout.
Scientific teams may feel constrained or less inspired by a less dramatic landing zone, creating some morale drag among exploration-focused personnel.
Resource upside is deferred, not lost; we accept a slower start on exotic extraction in exchange for stronger colony resilience.
Early effort and mass allocation to shielding and shelter may reduce visible expansion speed, but should preserve manpower and reduce catastrophic setbacks.
watchMetricsNextTurn: Landing injury count and EVA incident rate.
Days to establish baseline storm shelter and shielding coverage per colonist.
Water extraction rate from subsurface ice and purity stability.
Habitat construction speed on Arcadia terrain versus projections.
Radiation exposure per colonist and shelter attenuation performance.
Morale trend, especially among science and exploration crews.
Life-support stability: oxygen, water recycling, hygiene throughput.
Any delayed engineering assessment on terrain, power deployment, and construction bottlenecks.

policyEffectsApplied: Settle Arcadia Planitia as the primary landing and first permanent base site.
Declare Phase 1 construction priority: radiation shielding, storm shelter capacity, and habitat hardening.
Begin immediate subsurface ice survey and extraction setup to secure water and life-support inputs.
Restrict high-risk canyon and steep-slope operations until heavy rescue, medevac, and construction capability matures.
Establish strict EVA trauma prevention protocols from day one, including fall protection, route discipline, and rescue readiness.
Start continuous health monitoring for radiation exposure, bone density, and psychological stress at arrival.

departmentReports.summary: I recommend Option B: build the atmospheric water extraction system (WAVAR-type). Medically, the colony is already operating with a 20% water deficit, which the model classifies as moderate stress. That deficit threatens hydration, sanitation, food production, and psychological stability. Option A could restore water faster, but it introduces a meaningful contamination risk to pristine subsurface aquifers, which is a long-term planetary protection and public health concern. Option B is slower, but it reliably closes the deficit with lower ecological and medical downside. With 103 colonists, 800 L/day production versus 1000 L/day need, the shortfall is 200 L/day; per-capita availability is about 7.77 L/day now versus 9.71 L/day needed. That gap is enough to justify a stable, low-risk systems approach rather than a high-risk deep-drilling escalation.

citations.text: Mars subsurface ice confirmed at multiple latitudes by MARSIS and SHARAD radar
citations.context: Confirms that subsurface ice is real, but does not eliminate the risk that deeper drilling could encounter or contaminate isolated reservoirs.

citations.text: MOXIE on Perseverance demonstrated in-situ oxygen extraction from Mars atmosphere
citations.context: Supports the broader feasibility of atmospheric in-situ resource extraction technologies on Mars.

citations.text: Mars atmosphere contains 0.03% water vapor, seasonally variable
citations.context: Shows that atmospheric water is sparse but recoverable with engineered systems, matching the proposed WAVAR approach.
Relevant to the contamination risk posed by deep drilling into pristine subsurface aquifers.

risks.description: A persistent 20% water deficit raises dehydration risk, increases infection vulnerability through reduced hygiene, and can impair cognitive performance and morale.
Deep drilling could contaminate pristine aquifers with terrestrial biological material, creating irreversible scientific and health consequences.
A 6-month build time for atmospheric extraction prolongs the current deficit, requiring strict rationing and hygiene prioritization during transition.

opportunities.description: Atmospheric water extraction offers a reliable, lower-risk way to close the deficit without threatening subsurface ecosystems.
The colony can temporarily optimize water use, recycling, and agricultural priorities while the system is built.
If successful, the WAVAR system reduces dependence on uncertain subsurface drilling and improves long-term operational resilience.

recommendedActions: Choose Option B and begin immediate WAVAR-type system deployment.
Ration water aggressively during the 6-month build period, prioritizing drinking, sanitation, and life support.
Increase water recycling efficiency and suspend nonessential industrial water use.
Monitor colonists for dehydration, constipation, urinary issues, fatigue, and mood decline.
Avoid penetrating deeper aquifers until planetary protection and contamination controls are demonstrably robust.

forgedToolsUsed.name: water_stress_model_run
forgedToolsUsed.description: Computes the colony's water deficit severity and compares the health/mission tradeoff between experimental deep drilling and atmospheric water extraction.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Chen Osei
featuredColonistUpdates.update: No acute illness, but water restriction will be a key determinant of physical and cognitive performance over the next six months.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Liam Nkomo
featuredColonistUpdates.update: Higher water security from atmospheric extraction would better protect renal and gastrointestinal health than risky deep drilling.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Bruno Eriksson
featuredColonistUpdates.update: Psychological strain is likely to rise if the water deficit persists, so a stable systems solution is preferable.

openQuestions: What is the actual achievable yield of the WAVAR prototype at current temperatures and power availability?
How much water recycling efficiency can be improved in parallel with the new system?
Are there any signs of contamination already in the current drill network?

departmentReports.decision: Option B — Build the atmospheric water extraction (WAVAR-type) system
departmentReports.reasoning: The colony is currently producing 800 L/day against a 1000 L/day need, so this is a 20% shortfall. For a life-support critical resource, I prefer the lower-risk, lower-contamination path even if it is slower. The experimental deep drill offers faster upside, but it risks contaminating pristine subsurface aquifers and carries a significant power burden. Mars atmosphere is dry, but atmospheric water extraction is a proven engineering lineage and matches the actual deficit reliably. Since the gap is modest, a 6-month ramp is acceptable if paired with strict rationing and recovery efficiency improvements during deployment.
scienceBasis: Mars subsurface ice is real and valuable, but deeper aquifer uncertainty increases drilling risk.
Mars atmospheric water vapor is low (~0.03%) yet still technically harvestable with dedicated systems.
Planetary protection strongly disfavors contaminating pristine subsurface reservoirs when alternatives exist.

assessment.foodStatus: Reserves remain adequate at 18.8 months, but the 20% water shortfall is already constraining food production capacity and increases operational risk over time.
scienceBasis: Mars subsurface ice is real and geographically confirmed, but deeper drilling into pristine aquifers increases planetary-protection and contamination risk.
Mars atmospheric water vapor is sparse but usable with proven capture approaches; ISS-derived water recovery heritage supports Option B's technical credibility.
A 20% shortfall is not an emergency collapse state today, but it is a direct drag on hydroponics and food resilience.
assessment.rationale: Choose the atmospheric water extraction system (WAVAR-type). It is slower, but it is the lower-risk, scientifically grounded path that reliably closes the 20% deficit without risking contamination of pristine subsurface water reserves. Given food security depends on water and the colony still has sufficient reserves, reliability and planetary protection outweigh the faster but riskier deep-drilling payoff.
notes: Option A is only justified if water reserves become critically short before WAVAR deployment, or if atmospheric extraction underperforms in practice.
Maintain strict water rationing and prioritize agriculture until the additional supply comes online.

commanderDecision.decision: Choose Option B: deploy the atmospheric water extraction system immediately, while enforcing a sharp interim water austerity regime and accelerating recycling upgrades.
commanderDecision.rationale: We are under water stress, but this is exactly when reckless extraction can trap a young colony in a bigger future loss. Deep drilling offers speed, and I respect the upside, but contaminating a pristine aquifer this early would be a strategic blunder with scientific, ecological, and legitimacy costs that echo for decades. Option B is slower, cleaner, and more scalable. It preserves future deep-water options while solving the current deficit through engineered control rather than irreversible intrusion. This is the aggressive move in the long game: build a repeatable atmospheric water platform, squeeze efficiency hard for six months, and come out with a more resilient water architecture instead of a lucky shortcut. We will not drift through the transition. We will ration hard, protect core human needs, and treat every liter like reactor fuel.
selectedPolicies: Approve immediate construction of the WAVAR-type atmospheric water extraction system.
Enact temporary emergency water rationing for the 6-month build period.
Prioritize water allocation in this order: drinking, medical/sanitation, life support, food production, then all nonessential uses.
Mandate immediate water recycling efficiency upgrades and leak-loss audits across habitat systems.
Suspend or defer nonessential industrial and research activities with meaningful water consumption.
Impose a moratorium on deeper aquifer penetration until contamination controls and planetary protection protocols are proven.
rejectedPolicies: Option A: deep drilling into subsurface aquifers as the primary near-term solution.
Maintain normal water use during the extraction build period.
Allow discretionary industrial water consumption while the colony runs a 20% deficit.
Trade long-term aquifer integrity for short-term supply relief without robust contamination safeguards.
expectedTradeoffs: The colony endures 6 months of tighter rationing, which may depress morale and increase interpersonal friction.
Some research and industrial output will slow because water is being redirected to survival-critical functions.
Agricultural flexibility may narrow temporarily if water prioritization constrains crop operations.
In exchange, we avoid potentially irreversible aquifer contamination and gain a controlled, expandable water production pathway.
Short-term discomfort buys long-term strategic freedom: future subsurface extraction remains available once safeguards mature.
watchMetricsNextTurn: Daily net water balance: production, consumption, recycling recovery, and losses.
Per-capita water availability versus the 9.71 L/day requirement.
WAVAR construction progress against the 6-month schedule.
Hydration-related medical indicators: dehydration cases, urinary issues, constipation, fatigue, and infection rates.
Sanitation adequacy: hygiene access, waste-processing stability, and contamination incidents.
Morale trend under rationing, especially stress-linked conflict signals.
Agricultural water draw and any resulting effect on food growth projections.
Any signs of unauthorized drilling, protocol drift, or system leakage.

policyEffectsApplied: Approve immediate construction of the WAVAR-type atmospheric water extraction system.
Enact temporary emergency water rationing for the 6-month build period.
Prioritize water allocation in this order: drinking, medical/sanitation, life support, food production, then all nonessential uses.
Mandate immediate water recycling efficiency upgrades and leak-loss audits across habitat systems.
Suspend or defer nonessential industrial and research activities with meaningful water consumption.
Impose a moratorium on deeper aquifer penetration until contamination controls and planetary protection protocols are proven.

departmentReports.summary: I recommend Option A: full hydroponic conversion. The science is clear that Mars regolith is broadly unsuitable for direct food production because perchlorates are globally present, and the health risk is ingestion of contaminated crops leading to thyroid disruption. Bioremediation is promising in principle, but it is still unproven under Mars conditions and delays a safe food system by about two years. In contrast, sealed hydroponics are controllable, predictable, and medically safer despite the higher power demand. Given the colony’s current state — 113 people, 20 months of food reserve, 400 kW power, average radiation already at 1102 mSv, and bone loss beginning — I prefer the option that minimizes food-toxin exposure and avoids a prolonged period of uncertain agriculture. The forged risk model also favored hydroponics on operational safety grounds.

citations.text: Phoenix lander detected 0.5-1% calcium perchlorate in Mars soil globally
citations.context: Establishes that perchlorate contamination is a widespread Mars soil problem, not a local anomaly.

citations.text: Perchlorate is a thyroid toxin at chronic exposure above 0.7 ug/kg/day
citations.context: Supports the medical concern that contaminated crops could create chronic endocrine harm through ingestion.

citations.text: Perchlorate-reducing bacteria (Dechloromonas) can bioremediate contaminated soil
citations.context: Shows the concept is scientifically plausible, but still not the safest immediate colony choice.

citations.text: Bioremediation has not been tested in Mars atmospheric conditions
citations.context: Key counterpoint: the strategy is experimental and time-delayed under Mars-relevant conditions.

risks.description: Eating crops grown in untreated regolith could expose colonists to perchlorate-associated thyroid dysfunction, especially over long durations.
A 2-year bioremediation R&D program delays food safety improvements and prolongs dependence on a risky agricultural substrate.
Because the colony already has high cumulative radiation and early bone loss, any food system that risks chronic illness or underperformance compounds overall medical vulnerability.
Hydroponics requires 30% more power, which raises engineering strain, but this is a manageable systems risk compared with toxin exposure.

opportunities.description: Hydroponic bays provide fully controllable food production, enabling clean produce and tighter medical oversight.
Sealed agriculture can improve hygiene, biosecurity, and crop reliability in the long term.
Bioremediation research can continue in parallel at small scale without jeopardizing the primary food supply.

recommendedActions: Convert to full hydroponic agriculture now.
Reserve bioremediation for contained research plots only, not colony-wide food production.
Prioritize power allocation and thermal management for sealed grow bays.
Monitor thyroid function, nutritional status, and crop contamination continuously.
Use the contaminated regolith only for non-food engineering applications until remediation is proven safe.
Maintain strict food supply buffers to avoid the psychological stress of crop failure.

forgedToolsUsed.name: perchlorate_food_risk_model
forgedToolsUsed.description: Computes a simple risk comparison for Mars agriculture options under global perchlorate contamination, estimating thyroid-toxin exposure risk and operational burden for hydroponics versus bioremediation.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Chen Osei
featuredColonistUpdates.update: At current exposure levels, the biggest medical threat is not the existing radiation alone but avoidable nutritional and endocrine stress from unsafe food systems.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Liam Nkomo
featuredColonistUpdates.update: Hydroponic food security is likely to better support long-term endurance and recovery than experimental soil remediation.

featuredColonistUpdates.name: Luna Tanaka
featuredColonistUpdates.update: The safest psychological outcome is a predictable, contamination-free food supply rather than waiting on uncertain remediation.

openQuestions: How much of the 400 kW power budget can be dedicated to hydroponics without compromising life support and heating?
Can some hydroponic infrastructure be integrated with waste-heat recovery to reduce the 30% power penalty?
What isolated test protocol can be used for bioremediation research without risking the main food chain?

departmentReports.reasoning: Mars soil perchlorate contamination is a global, not site-specific, agricultural constraint. Phoenix data show 0.5–1% calcium perchlorate in regolith, which makes direct soil agriculture unsafe without remediation. While perchlorate-reducing bacteria are scientifically plausible, they are unproven under Mars conditions and impose a 2-year R&D delay before any colony-wide benefit. Given the colony’s food security role, I would not accept an unvalidated biological dependence for primary food production. Sealed hydroponics is mature, controllable, and avoids chronic toxin exposure risks to crew and crops. The power penalty is significant, but with 400 kW available and the colony already surviving on 120 kW life support capacity, the engineering cost is manageable compared with the risk of food failure.
scienceBasis: Phoenix detected widespread perchlorate contamination in Mars regolith.
Perchlorate is a thyroid toxin with meaningful chronic exposure risk.
Dechloromonas-based bioremediation is promising but not validated in Mars environmental conditions.
Hydroponic agriculture provides deterministic control of nutrients, contamination, and yield.
Phoenix detected 0.5-1% calcium perchlorate in Mars soil, and that level is globally distributed, so this is not a local remediation problem.
Perchlorate is a real chronic thyroid risk, making open-soil farming unsafe for routine food production without extensive treatment.
Hydroponics removes soil exposure entirely and is the only immediately controllable, evidence-backed path.
Perchlorate-reducing bacteria are scientifically plausible, but Mars atmospheric/soil performance is unproven and carries a 2-year delay.
reasoning: Food security priority is continuity, not speculative yield expansion.
The colony still has 20.0 months of reserves, so we can afford the transition to a safer system.
The colony’s 400 kW power budget can absorb a 30% increase to 120 kW for agriculture if managed carefully.
A failed bioremediation program would leave the colony with no safe food path and wasted time.
operationalRecommendation: Proceed with sealed hydroponic bays now.
Reserve contaminated regolith for future remediation research in isolated test beds only.
Do not commit colony-wide food production to engineered Dechloromonas until Mars-condition validation exists.
assessment.foodSecurityImpact: Hydroponic conversion preserves immediate production reliability and eliminates perchlorate exposure risk, protecting both human health and long-term food resilience.
assessment.status: Green for transition, caution on power allocation and material logistics.

commanderDecision.decision: Choose Option A: execute a full conversion to sealed hydroponic agriculture immediately, while isolating regolith cultivation to tightly contained research only.
commanderDecision.rationale: This is a clean command call. We do not gamble the colony’s metabolism on contaminated dirt. Perchlorates turn regolith farming from a frontier dream into a slow poison unless and until remediation is proven under Martian conditions. Hydroponics costs more power, yes—but power strain is an engineering problem. Chronic food toxicity is a civilization-killer. With cumulative radiation already punishing the population and bone loss beginning, the colony cannot afford another invisible biological drag. We need food systems that are controllable, measurable, and fast to secure. Hydroponics gives us a sealed production chain, rapid quality assurance, and room to optimize yield aggressively. Bioremediation still has value, but as a contained R&D flank, not as the backbone of colony nutrition. We push certainty into the food supply now and buy time to innovate safely later.
selectedPolicies: Approve immediate full-scale transition to sealed hydroponic food production.
Ban colony-wide use of untreated regolith for edible crop cultivation.
Limit bioremediation to controlled experimental plots with strict containment and validation gates.
Reallocate power budget to support hydroponic grow bays, water circulation, lighting, and thermal control.
Establish continuous food safety screening for perchlorate contamination, nutrient quality, and system pathogens.
Classify contaminated regolith as non-food-use material for construction and industrial applications only.
Maintain and protect a strategic food buffer during the conversion period to absorb any production instability.
rejectedPolicies: Pursue bioremediation as the primary near-term food strategy.
Continue or expand edible crop production in untreated regolith.
Accept a 2-year delay in food safety improvements while remediation remains unproven on Mars.
Trade predictable power strain for uncertain long-term toxin exposure.
expectedTradeoffs: Power demand will rise sharply, increasing pressure on generation capacity, maintenance cycles, and load management.
Engineering flexibility may shrink in the short term as electricity is diverted from other projects into food security.
Hydroponic infrastructure is more equipment-intensive and vulnerable to mechanical failure than simple soil cultivation, demanding tighter operations discipline.
The colony gains a safer, more controllable food system and sharply reduces the risk of chronic perchlorate exposure.
Bioremediation progress may slow as it is confined to research lanes, but this preserves safety while still developing a future expansion option.
watchMetricsNextTurn: Power margin after hydroponic conversion: peak load, reserve capacity, and outage frequency.
Hydroponic crop yield per kW and per liter of water.
Food reserve trend during transition versus the 20-month baseline.
Perchlorate screening results in all edible outputs.
Thyroid markers, nutritional status, and other chronic exposure indicators across the population.
System reliability in grow bays: pump failures, contamination events, thermal excursions, and lighting downtime.
Morale response to another restrictive but safety-driven policy shift.
Bioremediation research progress and evidence threshold for any future limited expansion.

policyEffectsApplied: Approve immediate full-scale transition to sealed hydroponic food production.
Ban colony-wide use of untreated regolith for edible crop cultivation.
Limit bioremediation to controlled experimental plots with strict containment and validation gates.
Reallocate power budget to support hydroponic grow bays, water circulation, lighting, and thermal control.
Establish continuous food safety screening for perchlorate contamination, nutrient quality, and system pathogens.
Classify contaminated regolith as non-food-use material for construction and industrial applications only.
Maintain and protect a strategic food buffer during the conversion period to absorb any production instability.
